lesson_title: Why functions are useful, and how to use them effectively

cards.explanation:
Functions provide key benefits:
  • Organization: Break large programs into manageable pieces
  • Reusability: Write once, use many times
  • Testing: Easier to test individual functions
  • Readability: Good names make code self-documenting
  • Maintainability: Fix bugs in one place
examples: int max(int a, int b) { return (a > b) ? a : b; }

cards.front: What is the DRY principle and how do functions help?
cards.back: DRY stands for "Don't Repeat Yourself". Functions help by allowing you to write code once and reuse it, rather than copying and pasting.

cards.text: Functions should ideally do {{c1::one}} thing well. A function that does too many things is harder to {{c2::understand}}, {{c3::test}}, and {{c4::reuse}}.

cards.front: Should function names describe what the function does or how it does it?
cards.back: Function names should describe what the function does, not how it does it. This makes code more readable and maintainable.

cards.explanation:
Effective function names:
  • Use verbs for action functions: calculateTotal(), printReport()
  • Be specific and descriptive: getUserInput() not get()
  • Match the level of abstraction
  • Avoid abbreviations unless widely known

cards.front: How long should a function typically be?
cards.back: Functions should be as short as practical - typically a few lines to a screenful. If a function is too long, consider breaking it into smaller helper functions.

cards.text: Code that appears in more than one place should usually be made into a {{c1::function}} to avoid {{c2::duplication}}.

cards.front: What are the benefits of breaking a large function into smaller functions?
cards.back:
Smaller functions are:
  • Easier to understand
  • Easier to test
  • More reusable
  • Easier to debug
  • Less prone to errors

cards.front: Why are functions easier to test than large blocks of code?
cards.back: Functions can be tested in isolation with specific inputs and expected outputs, making it easier to verify correctness and find bugs.
A helper function is a small function that performs a specific sub-task for a larger function, improving code organization and reusability.
